event: Scenes from the Passion of Christ — four-panel altarpiece
symbols: garden at night — Gethsemane, the place of agony and betrayal
torches and weapons — the hostile forces arresting the Prince of Peace
Christ in white — innocence and purity amid the violence of the Passion
four panels — the sequential unfolding of the Passion narrative
description: Four panels arranged in a grid showing scenes from Christ's Passion. Upper left: a night scene in a garden with sleeping figures and dramatic fire or celestial light (Gethsemane). Upper right: a chaotic crowd scene with soldiers carrying torches and weapons, banners flying (the Arrest). Lower left: Christ in a white robe being mocked or tried in an interior with architectural details. Lower right: a crowded interior scene with many figures, possibly the trial before Pilate or the flagellation.
theological_description: This four-panel Passion cycle by Altdorfer compresses the central drama of Christian salvation into a unified visual narrative. The sequence moves from Gethsemane — where Christ prayed 'Not my will but yours be done' (Luke 22:42) — through the arrest with its torches and swords, to the trials and abuse before the authorities. Each panel represents a station in Christ's willing journey toward the cross, fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy: 'He was oppressed, and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth; like a lamb that is led to the slaughter' (Isa 53:7). The night settings of the upper panels emphasize the spiritual darkness enveloping the world as its Creator is seized: 'This is your hour, and the power of darkness' (Luke 22:53). The lower panels bring the action indoors, where religious and political authority conspire to condemn the innocent one. The Passion narrative is the theological core of Christianity — the cross is not merely a tragic death but God's chosen means of reconciling the world to himself (2 Cor 5:19).

scripture_refs.reason: The agony in Gethsemane — the upper left panel showing prayer and sleeping disciples
The arrest of Jesus with swords and clubs — the upper right panel
The trial before the high priest — one of the lower panels
Led like a lamb to slaughter — the Suffering Servant enduring the Passion
This is your hour and the power of darkness — the night settings of the arrest
God was in Christ reconciling the world — the redemptive purpose behind the Passion
